title: Tie a Secure Knot
summary: Tie a basic knot that holds securely for everyday uses.
learnerPromise: You will be able to tie a simple, secure knot that stays tight when you need it to and unties when you want it to.
whyItMatters: A good knot helps you secure items, tie bags closed, fasten ropes, and handle simple tasks at home or outdoors. It is a small skill that comes in surprisingly often.
realLifeUses: Tying a rope to secure items on a bike or cart
Closing a bag or bundle tightly so nothing falls out
Fastening a line to a post or railing for temporary use
Helping set up a tent or tarp with secure guy lines
materialsNeeded: A piece of rope, cord, or sturdy string at least 2 feet long
result: A securely tied knot that holds under light tension.
youWillLearn: How to tie a basic overhand or reef knot
How to tighten a knot so it does not slip loose
How to untie a knot without cutting the rope
How to choose a knot for the task at hand
miniChallenge: Using a piece of string or cord, tie a secure knot around a fixed object like a chair leg or handle. Pull on it to check that it holds. Then untie it without cutting.
steps: Hold the two ends of the rope you want to join or fasten.
Cross the right end over the left end and tuck it underneath.
Cross the left end over the new right end and tuck it underneath again.
Pull both ends slowly to tighten the knot evenly.
Test the knot with a gentle pull. To untie, pull one of the loose ends free.
completionCriteria: You tied a knot that held securely when pulled gently.
You were able to untie it without cutting the rope.
commonProblems: The knot slips loose because it was not tightened enough
Creating a granny knot by mistake, which unties more easily
Pulling too hard and jamming the knot so it is hard to untie
tips: Right over left, then left over right makes a reef knot that holds well.
Wet or slippery rope may need an extra loop to stay tight.
Leave short tails on the ends so the knot does not slip out.
Practice on a thick, easy-to-handle cord first.
